chunk_summary: Nasogastric feeding is essential for malnourished children with persistent diarrhea when oral intake is inadequate (<80% of prescribed volume for 2–3 feeds). Safety protocols include trained staff, tube position confirmation, and gradual de-escalation based on oral intake recovery and weight gain.
learning_objectives: Understand triggers for initiating nasogastric feeding in malnourished children with persistent diarrhea.
Learn safety protocols for nasogastric tube insertion and feeding management.
Recognize criteria for transitioning from nasogastric feeding to oral intake.
Identify risks such as aspiration and feeding aversion in severely malnourished children.
key_facts: Nasogastric feeding is initiated when oral intake is <80% of prescribed volume for 2–3 consecutive feeds.
Safe feeding techniques include gravity or gentle pressure feeding and avoiding bottle feeding due to aspiration risk.
Tube position must be confirmed before each feed according to local protocols.
De-escalation criteria: oral intake ≥80% of target across a full day or two consecutive full feeds, with weight gain and no vomiting or respiratory symptoms.
Prolonged tube feeding or recurrent aspiration requires specialist review for underlying neurologic or structural issues.
reference_quote: Offer the stabilization feed orally first at each feed, then use a nasogastric tube when the child cannot take enough orally. A minimum acceptable intake is about 80% of the prescribed volume; if the child takes <80% for 2–3 consecutive feeds, initiate NG supplementation rather than waiting for appetite recovery. (National Health Mission)
document_key_topics: Nasogastric feeding triggers in malnourished children
Criteria for transitioning from tube feeding to oral intake
Aspiration risk and feeding aversion management
